concerns.text: Tasks T030 and T031 implement a Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) method for dipole/quadrupole extraction. The Spec (FR-004) and Plan explicitly mandate ONLY the pseudo-C_l method with MASTER correction. There is no FR or SC in the spec authorizing an MLE implementation, making these tasks unbacked by requirements and creating implementation waste.

Tasks T038 and T039 implement Gaussian Random Field (GRF) simulations for the null distribution. The Spec (FR-005) and Plan explicitly mandate generating null catalogs via random 3D rotation matrices. There is no FR or SC authorizing GRF generation, making these tasks unbacked by requirements.

Task T022 (selection bias metrics) and T043 (calibration drift test) lack explicit mapping to any Functional Requirement (FR) or Success Criterion (SC). While SC-003 mentions stability, it refers to the running mean of simulated amplitudes, not a specific 'calibration drift' test or 'selection bias metrics' calculation. These tasks appear to be gold-plating without a defined measurement protocol in the spec.

Task T025 validates the unapproved MLE implementation. Task T032 attempts to compare MLE results against pseudo-C_l results to satisfy SC-002. However, SC-002 requires validation against a 'canonical implementation' of the approved method (pseudo-C_l), not a comparison between a rejected method and the approved one. These tasks do not correctly map to the intent of SC-002.

Task T018 instructs the implementer to 'Update spec.md' to add a mandatory redshift range. This is a stale tag from an older workflow; tasks cannot modify the Spec (the source of truth). The redshift range requirement should exist in the Spec before the tasks are generated, or be handled via a formal amendment process, not as a task to rewrite the spec file.

Task T020 requires documenting the photometric system in comments. While good practice, there is no specific FR or SC in the spec mandating this specific documentation step. It is a minor writing-level gap in traceability, though the intent (systematics handling) is covered by the broader US1 scope.
